players.name: Sergio Rochet
players.bio: Uruguay's first-choice keeper, Rochet has been a reliable last line of defence for La Celeste. The Internacional stopper earned his place through consistent club form and composed shot-stopping at international level.
players.facts: Born just one day before teammate Guillermo Varela — the squad's most convenient birthday party combo.

players.name: José María Giménez
players.bio: One cap shy of a century, Giménez is Uruguay's defensive rock at Atlético Madrid. A commanding centre-back with a taste for goals, he was sensational at the 2022 World Cup and arrives at 2026 as a seasoned leader.
players.facts: Has scored 8 international goals — remarkable for a centre-back who isn't supposed to be up there.

players.name: Sebastián Cáceres
players.bio: Young and composed, Cáceres has established himself as a dependable option in Uruguay's back line. The América defender brings athleticism and positional intelligence to a competitive defensive unit.
players.facts: At just 26 with 24 caps, Cáceres could be Uruguay's defensive cornerstone well into the 2030s.

players.name: Ronald Araújo
players.bio: A colossus at the heart of Barcelona's defence, Araújo is built like a tank and plays like one too. Rapid, powerful and fearless, the Uruguayan has become one of Europe's premier centre-backs.
players.facts: Ran the 100m in 10.6 seconds — making him one of the fastest defenders on the planet. Defenders aren't meant to do that.

players.name: Manuel Ugarte
players.bio: Manchester United's midfield enforcer, Ugarte is one of football's finest ball-winners. Tenacious, intelligent and seemingly everywhere at once, the 25-year-old is Uruguay's defensive midfield engine.
players.facts: Made more ball recoveries than almost any midfielder in Europe — opponents basically just hand it to him at this point.

players.name: Rodrigo Bentancur
players.bio: A cultured Tottenham midfielder with 74 caps, Bentancur brings composure and range to Uruguay's engine room. Graduated from Juventus's famed academy and has been a La Celeste regular for nearly a decade.
players.facts: Was signed by Juventus after being recommended by club legend Carlos Tévez. Some references carry weight.

players.name: Nicolás de la Cruz
players.full_name: Diego Nicolás de la Cruz Arcosa
players.bio: The Flamengo playmaker is a creative force with an eye for goal from midfield. De la Cruz has flourished in Brazil's Brasileirão and brings flair and technical quality to Uruguay's attacking play.
players.facts: Won the Copa Libertadores with Flamengo — South America's biggest club prize. Not bad for a lad from Montevideo.

players.name: Federico Valverde
players.bio: One of Real Madrid's most important players, Valverde is a box-to-box powerhouse with an eye for a spectacular goal. 'El Pajarito' (The Little Bird) has enormous wings — and the Champions League medals to prove it.
players.facts: Scored a stunning long-range goal in the 2022 Copa del Rey final. Real Madrid fans still talk about it constantly.

players.name: Darwin Núñez
players.bio: A raw, explosive striker who terrorises defenders with his pace and physicality. Núñez moved to Al-Hilal after Liverpool and arrives at 2026 hungry to prove himself on the world's biggest stage.
players.facts: Cost Liverpool £85m in 2022 — one of the most expensive transfers in history. No pressure, Darwin.

players.name: Giorgian de Arrascaeta
players.bio: Uruguay's No.10 and heartbeat of Flamengo's attack, De Arrascaeta is a technically gifted playmaker from tiny Nuevo Berlín. A two-time Copa Libertadores winner, he carries the weight of expectation beautifully.
players.facts: Grew up in Nuevo Berlín, a Uruguayan town founded by German immigrants — his name is Italian. Uruguay contains multitudes.

players.name: Facundo Pellistri
players.bio: A pacy, direct winger who developed at Manchester United before finding his footing in Europe, Pellistri is one of Uruguay's most exciting wide options. Quick feet and a fearless attitude make him a handful.
players.facts: Was signed by Manchester United aged 18 on transfer deadline day 2020 — Ole Gunnar Solskjær's scouts clearly liked what they saw.

players.name: Santiago Mele
players.bio: Mele is Uruguay's second-choice keeper bringing Concacaf experience from Monterrey. With only 8 caps, the 28-year-old is still building his international career but is reliable cover behind Rochet.
players.facts: Plays his club football in Mexico — giving him a home crowd advantage at a World Cup hosted partly in the USA and Mexico.

players.name: Guillermo Varela
players.bio: A seasoned right-back who has played in Europe and Brazil's Série A with Flamengo, Varela is an experienced and dependable option for Uruguay. At 33, 2026 may well be his World Cup swansong.
players.facts: Born on 24 March 1993 — just one day after teammate Sergio Rochet. The squad's most underrated coincidence.

players.name: Agustín Canobbio
players.bio: A versatile and energetic winger who has made his mark in Brazil with Fluminense, Canobbio offers creativity and directness from wide positions. Still establishing himself internationally with 15 caps.
players.facts: Plays in the same Fluminense squad that won the Copa Libertadores in 2023 — he knows what winning looks like.

players.name: Emiliano Martínez
players.bio: Not to be confused with Argentina's famous Emiliano Martínez, this goalkeeper was born in Mar del Plata but pledged allegiance to Uruguay. The Palmeiras stopper is a capable third-choice option.
players.facts: Born in Argentina but plays for Uruguay — the paperwork confusion at passport control must be spectacular.

players.name: Mathías Olivera
players.bio: Napoli's attack-minded left-back, Olivera is known for his overlapping runs and delivery from wide areas. A physically imposing full-back who has become a key part of Uruguay's defensive setup.
players.facts: Joined Napoli the same summer they won Serie A in 2023 — arriving at a club at just the right moment.

players.name: Matías Viña
players.bio: A marauding left-back with 43 caps, Viña has experience across Serie A and now River Plate. Comfortable going forward, he provides width and energy down Uruguay's left flank.
players.facts: Has represented Uruguay at every level from youth to senior — a true product of the Uruguayan football system.

players.name: Brian Rodríguez
players.bio: A pacy, direct forward who plies his trade with Club América in Mexico, Brian Rodríguez has pace to burn and four international goals to his name. The 26-year-old adds depth and dynamism to Uruguay's attack.
players.facts: Previously played in MLS with LA FC — so he knows the North American venues of this World Cup better than most.

players.name: Rodrigo Aguirre
players.bio: A clinical striker who has found the net regularly for UANL in Mexico's Liga MX, Aguirre brings veteran experience to Uruguay's forward line. Three goals from just 10 caps shows an impressive conversion rate.
players.facts: Scored 3 goals in 10 caps — a better international ratio than many far more celebrated strikers. Efficiency is underrated.

players.name: Maximiliano Araújo
players.bio: A dynamic and technically gifted midfielder at Sporting CP, Maximiliano Araújo — no relation to Ronald — offers Uruguay creativity and versatility. Three goals from 28 caps show he can contribute at both ends.
players.facts: Shares a surname with teammate Ronald Araújo but they are not related — a fact Uruguayan journalists never tire of clarifying.

players.name: Federico Viñas
players.bio: A forward with an eye for goal, Viñas plays his football with Oviedo in Spain's second tier. With 11 caps and 2 international goals, he offers a different attacking option off the bench for La Celeste.
players.facts: Plays in Spain's Segunda División — making him one of very few World Cup players from outside the top flight.

players.name: Joaquín Piquerez
players.bio: A left-sided player who has carved out a role at Copa Libertadores-winning Palmeiras, Piquerez brings Brazilian club pedigree to Uruguay's squad. Versatile and hardworking across the left flank.
players.facts: Won the Copa Libertadores with Palmeiras — South America's Champions League. Not many World Cup players can say that.

players.name: Fernando Muslera
players.bio: A Uruguayan legend with 134 caps, Muslera has been La Celeste's guardian for nearly two decades. At 39, the Galatasaray great's World Cup career has spanned four tournaments — a true icon of the game.
players.facts: With 134 caps, Muslera is one of Uruguay's most-capped players ever. He's been keeping goal since some teammates were in primary school.

players.name: Santiago Bueno
players.bio: A solid and dependable centre-back at Wolverhampton Wanderers, Bueno has earned his place in the Premier League through sheer determination. Still establishing himself internationally with 8 caps at 27.
players.facts: Plays in the Premier League with Wolves — giving him top-flight European experience that few Uruguayan defenders possess.

players.name: Juan Manuel Sanabria
players.bio: A creative midfielder who has made his mark in MLS with Real Salt Lake, Sanabria is one of the fresher faces in Uruguay's squad with just 5 caps. A goal already suggests he knows how to make an impression.
players.facts: Plays in MLS — meaning this World Cup, partly held in the USA, is practically a home tournament for him logistically.

players.name: Rodrigo Zalazar
players.bio: A technically accomplished midfielder now at Braga in Portugal, Zalazar has built his career across Europe and brings energy and an eye for goal to Uruguay's squad. Two international goals from 8 caps is an encouraging return.
players.facts: Born in Uruguay, developed in Germany with Schalke, now plays in Portugal — a true citizen of European football.
